answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, the Urdu word *'umr'* (عُمْر) – appearing in transliterated form as "umr" – consistently signifies "lifetime," "life," or "age," evoking the full span of human existence. It carries philosophical weight in his ghazals, often highlighting themes of prolonged suffering in love (*ishq*), the futility or transience of mortal vows and desires, separation (*furqat*), and the inexorable passage of time amid unfulfilled longing.[^1][^2] Ghalib uses it hyperbolically to contrast eternal emotions or cosmic delays with life's brevity, amplifying the lover's existential despair.

Key examples include:

- The iconic opening couplet of ghazal 3 equates a lover's sigh (*aah*) with something requiring an entire lifetime to manifest its effect: "Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak" (A sigh must wait an age to take its flight).[^3]
- In ghazal 27, a beloved's lifelong pledge of fidelity (*paimaan-e-wafa*) is dismissed as illusory, since "Umr bhar ka tu ne paimaan-e-wafa baandha to kya / Umr ko bhi to nahin hai paayedaari" (You swore a vow of faith for life, but pray, / Does life itself not promise to decay?). Here, *umr bhar* underscores life's inherent impermanence.[^4]
- Ghazal 70 laments tears shed "umr bhar" (through all my life of pain), portraying lifelong blood-like weeping from the eyes as a damning mark on the slayer: "Woh khoon jo chashm-e-tar se umr bhar yun dam-ba-dam nikle" (When from my weeping eyes, through all my life of pain, / A constant stream of blood would always flow).[^5]

These motifs recur across other couplets (e.g., ghazals 9, 40, 68), where *umr* intensifies irony: lifelong turmoil yields no resolution, and even extended life (*umr-e-Khizr*, alluding to the immortal Khidr) brings no escape from love's torment.[^6]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 3 ("Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak"), transliteration (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/transliteration/).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 27 ("Dard se mere hai tujh ko be-qarari haaye haaye"), translation (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/27-dard-se-mere-hai-tujh-ko-be-qarari-haaye-haaye/translation/).

[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 3, translation, [p-0733e9](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/translation/#p-0733e9); transliteration, [p-bcdca7](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/transliteration/#p-bcdca7).

[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 27, translation, [p-efe750](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/27-dard-se-mere-hai-tujh-ko-be-qarari-haaye-haaye/translation/#p-efe750); transliteration, [p-a71fc7](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/27-dard-se-mere-hai-tujh-ko-be-qarari-haaye-haaye/transliteration/#p-a71fc7).

[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 70 ("Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle"), translation, [p-9bc9b2](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-9bc9b2); transliteration, [p-6898cb](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/transliteration/#p-6898cb).

[^6]: E.g., ch. 68, transliteration (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/68-hareef-e-matlab-e-mushkil-nahin-fusoon-e-niyaaz/transliteration/#p-f62532).
